patterns: Best way to get things done
responses: Try the Pomodoro Technique: work for 25 minutes, then take a 5-minute break. Repeat 4 times, then take a longer break!
Tip: Write down your top 3 Most Important Tasks (MITs) each morning and focus on those first.
Remove distractions: put your phone in another room, use website blockers, and set a timer for deep work sessions.
Start with the hardest task first thing in the morning — this is called 'Eating the Frog'. The rest of the day feels easy after!
Plan tomorrow the night before. Spend 5 minutes each evening reviewing what you did and writing tomorrow's to-do list.
Here's a simple daily structure: Morning — review goals & exercise. Midday — deep work sessions. Afternoon — meetings & emails. Evening — review progress & plan tomorrow.
A great morning routine: 1) Wake up early, 2) Hydrate, 3) Exercise for 20min, 4) Write 3 priorities, 5) Start your most important task!
Try time-blocking: assign specific tasks to specific time slots in your calendar. This reduces decision fatigue and keeps you on track.

Top study tip: Use active recall — instead of re-reading, close the book and write down everything you remember. This boosts retention dramatically!
Try the Feynman Technique: explain the concept you're studying as if you're teaching it to a 5-year-old. If you can't, you need to study that part more.
Spaced repetition is key! Study material, then review it after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, and 1 month. This locks it into long-term memory.
Study in chunks of 25-45 minutes with short breaks. Avoid studying for hours straight — your brain needs rest to consolidate memories.
The three pillars of health: quality sleep (7-8 hours), regular movement (30+ min daily), and whole foods. Start with one!
For better sleep: keep a consistent wake time every day, avoid screens 1 hour before bed, and keep your room cool and dark.
Even a 30-minute walk daily can dramatically improve your energy, mood, and focus. Small consistent steps win!
Stay hydrated! Drink at least 8 glasses of water a day. Dehydration causes fatigue, poor focus, and headaches.
